text: Adults aged 40 to 75 years with no history of cardiovascular disease who have at least 1 CVD risk factor (dyslipidemia, diabetes, hypertension, or smoking) and an estimated 10-year CVD risk of 10% or greater should initiate a statin.
For patients with clinical ASCVD, high-intensity statin therapy is recommended with the goal of reducing LDL-C by 50% or more.
An HbA1c >= 6.5% is diagnostic of diabetes when confirmed on a repeat sample. Values 5.7-6.4% indicate prediabetes.
Adults 18 years or older should be screened for hypertension at every clinical encounter; positive screens should be confirmed with out-of-office BP measurements before initiating treatment.
Metformin remains the preferred initial pharmacologic agent for type 2 diabetes in patients without contraindications, owing to efficacy, low cost, and safety profile.
For most adults with hypertension, a target office BP of <130/80 mm Hg is recommended; treatment intensification is indicated when readings exceed this threshold on confirmed measurements.

Initiating low-dose aspirin for the primary prevention of CVD in adults 60 years or older has no net benefit. The decision to initiate aspirin in adults 40-59 with 10-year CVD risk >=10% should be individualized.

section: Glycemic targets in pharmacologically treated diabetes
text: An HbA1c target of <7% is reasonable for most non-pregnant adults with diabetes; less stringent targets (e.g. <8%) may be appropriate for patients with limited life expectancy or extensive comorbidities.

When intake forms list 'shellfish' or 'iodine' as an allergy without a confirmed reaction, document the verbatim text and surface to the clinician — iodinated contrast hypersensitivity is not predicted by shellfish allergy and the entries should not be conflated.

For triglycerides 175-499 mg/dL, lifestyle interventions (weight loss, decreased simple-sugar intake, increased physical activity) are first-line; pharmacotherapy is considered for triglycerides >=500 mg/dL.
After statin initiation, repeat lipid panel in 4-12 weeks to assess response and adherence; subsequent monitoring at 3-12 month intervals is reasonable for stable patients.

In adults with type 2 diabetes, additional CVD risk-enhancing factors (e.g. albuminuria, eGFR <60, retinopathy) should prompt earlier and more aggressive lipid-lowering therapy regardless of baseline 10-year ASCVD risk.
